completion.thinkerBio: Antonio Gramsci (1891–1937) was an Italian Marxist theorist who developed hegemony while imprisoned by Mussolini; his Prison Notebooks (written 1929–1935, published 1947–51) constitute one of the most original contributions to twentieth-century political philosophy.
completion.quote: The dominant class does not merely rule — it leads. Hegemony is leadership by consent rather than domination by force.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Both Gramsci's hegemony and Foucault's power-knowledge show how power operates through the production and naturalization of knowledge rather than direct coercion.

related.name: Ideology and False Consciousness
related.note: Gramsci's hegemony and Althusser's ideological state apparatuses both explain how the dominant class maintains power through civil society and culture.

related.name: Critical Theory
related.note: Frankfurt School critical theory addresses the same puzzle as Gramsci — why the working class does not revolt against capitalism despite its interests.

related.name: Deliberative Democracy
related.note: Gramsci's analysis of manufactured consent challenges deliberative democracy's assumption that genuine rational consensus is achievable in unequal societies.

books.title: Selections from the Prison Notebooks
books.author: Antonio Gramsci
books.why: The source — Gramsci's development of hegemony, the organic intellectual, and the war of position.

books.title: Hegemony and Socialist Strategy
books.author: Laclau and Mouffe
books.why: The post-Marxist extension of Gramsci's hegemony concept to contemporary radical politics.

books.title: Prison Notebooks
books.author: Antonio Gramsci (complete)
books.why: The complete notebooks — essential for understanding Gramsci's full political philosophy.

completion.name: Civil Disobedience
completion.thinkerBio: Henry David Thoreau (1817–1862) coined the term in 'Resistance to Civil Government' (1849); Mahatma Gandhi (1869–1948) and Martin Luther King Jr. (1929–1968) transformed it into a mass political practice that changed history in India and the American South.
completion.quote: Under a government which imprisons any unjustly, the true place for a just man is also a prison.

related.name: Nonviolence as Philosophy
related.note: Civil disobedience requires nonviolence as both a tactical and a philosophical commitment — Gandhi and King grounded it in a metaphysics of moral transformation.

related.name: Political Obligation
related.note: Civil disobedience is the most philosophically interesting test of political obligation — compliance with a higher law combined with acknowledgment of the rule of law.

related.name: Anarchism
related.note: Civil disobedience and anarchism share the rejection of unjust positive law — they differ on whether reform or abolition of the state is the goal.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Thoreau, Gandhi, and King all analyze civil disobedience as a response to structural power — not just individual injustice but systemic domination.

books.title: On the Duty of Civil Disobedience
books.author: Henry David Thoreau
books.why: The foundational text — Thoreau's argument that individual conscience supersedes positive law.

books.title: Letter from Birmingham Jail
books.author: Martin Luther King Jr.
books.why: The most powerful philosophical and theological defence of civil disobedience in the English language.

books.title: Hind Swaraj
books.author: Mahatma Gandhi
books.why: Gandhi's philosophical foundation for satyagraha — the metaphysics of nonviolent civil disobedience.

completion.name: Biopower
completion.thinkerBio: Michel Foucault (1926–1984) was a French philosopher and historian of ideas whose Discipline and Punish (1975) and The History of Sexuality Vol. 1 (1976) developed the concept of biopower; his Collège de France lectures elaborated its biopolitical dimensions.
completion.quote: The old power of death now gives way to the administration of bodies and the calculated management of life.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Biopower is one of the most important forms of Foucauldian power-knowledge — it produces subjects and populations through medical, administrative, and statistical knowledge.

related.name: Biopolitics and Bare Life
related.note: Agamben extends Foucault's biopower through bare life — sovereignty operates by deciding which lives can be exposed to death.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Both Gramsci's hegemony and Foucault's biopower explain how modern power operates through internalization and consent rather than direct coercion.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Foucault explicitly contrasted biopower with sovereign power — sovereignty 'lets live or makes die'; biopower 'makes live and lets die.'

books.title: Discipline and Punish
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: Foucault's analysis of disciplinary power — the panopticon and the production of docile bodies.

books.title: The History of Sexuality, Vol. 1
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: The explicit introduction of biopower — how the administration of sexuality became a site of political power.

books.title: Biopolitics: An Advanced Introduction
books.author: Thomas Lemke
books.why: The clearest contemporary overview of biopower and biopolitics across Foucault, Agamben, and contemporary theory.
